title: Signals and Systems
summary: This page contains end-of-chapter problems for Chapter 1 on Signals and Systems. Problems cover system properties including homogeneity and additivity, zero-state and zero-input linearity, causality vs. noncausality, and invertibility of systems described by various input-output relationships.

figures.caption: A circuit with three matched diodes, a capacitor C, and resistors 2R, 2R, and R, driven by input x(t) with output current y(t), used to demonstrate zero-state linearity but not zero-input linearity.
figures.description: The figure is a circuit diagram with no coordinate axes, showing an input voltage source labeled x(t) on the left and an output current labeled y(t) along the top wire. Three vertical branches connect the top and bottom rails: the left and middle branches each contain a matched diode above a 2R resistor, while the right branch contains a matched diode above a resistor R. A capacitor C bridges the midpoints of the left and middle branches, with capacitor voltage v_c labeled positive on the left plate and negative on the right plate. Students should notice the symmetry of the two 2R branches, the different rightmost R branch, and that the output is a current while the capacitor state can affect zero-input behavior.

figures.caption: A ladder network circuit with a current source input x(t), a parallel LC tank circuit, a 1 F capacitor, a 0.1 H inductor, and a 2 Ω resistor producing output voltage y(t).
figures.description: The figure is a circuit schematic with no plotted axes, showing an input current source labeled x(t) on the left feeding a ladder network and an output voltage labeled y(t) measured at the right with polarity marked + and −. The first section is a parallel LC branch labeled C and L connected between the input node and the next node, followed by a shunt 1 F capacitor to the bottom reference line, then a series 0.1 H inductor leading to the output node. A 2 Ω resistor connects the output node to the bottom reference line, so students should notice the series-shunt ladder structure and how the output voltage is taken across the resistor node relative to ground.
